title: Guard provider identity at the data layer, not the routes
context:
In April 2026, provider identity exposed a recurring failure mode: a component reported failure even when the lifecycle had objectively succeeded. The measured impact was real — the failure reproduced on the first end-to-end run but on no unit test.

The adopted fix was to serialize the conflicting writes through one owner so the shared resource has a single writer, implemented with Cytoscape + fcose. That keeps the contract in one place and makes the behaviour identical across every entry point, instead of re-deriving it at each call site where the copies inevitably diverge.

This class keeps recurring, so the durable lesson is blunt: one homogeneous list gets one table; heterogeneous settings each get their own card. The strongest evidence came from a clean-room check against the freshly published artifact rather than the local tree, which caught wiring bugs that isolated unit tests never saw because the production plumbing was wrong rather than the local logic.
consequences:
Provider identity must follow this rule at every entry point. Any path that splits the same validation across layers is a regression, because it quietly breaks the guarantee even when a formal check still returns a verdict.

Treat the extra work as the cost of the check — a second, independent perspective on cheap work is still worth more than a fast wrong answer. When auditing provider identity, first ask whether the invariant actually held; if it did not, treat the stage as missing its value even though it produced output.
crux: Provider identity is cheaper to verify end-to-end than to debug after it ships through Cytoscape + fcose.
